Q001: Under the Companies Act 2013, Section 166, a director has the following key duties: (1) Act in accordance with the articles of association of the company; (2) Act in good faith to promote the objects of the company for the benefit of its members as a whole, and in the best interests of the company, its employees, shareholders, the community and for the protection of the environment; (3) Exercise duties with due and reasonable care, skill and diligence, and exercise independent judgment; (4) Not involve in situations where there is a direct or indirect conflict of interest with the company; (5) Not achieve or attempt to achieve any undue gain or advantage to himself, his relatives, partners or associates; (6) Not assign his office to any other person. A director who contravenes these provisions shall be liable to a penalty of not less than one lakh rupees and not more than five lakh rupees.
Q002: Under Section 42 of the Companies Act 2013, a private placement is an offer of securities or invitation to subscribe securities to a select group of persons (other than by way of public offer). Key conditions include: (1) No offer or invitation may be made to more than two hundred persons in the aggregate in a financial year; (2) The offer must be made only to persons whose names are recorded by the company prior to the invitation; (3) No fresh offer shall be made unless the allotments with respect to any preceding offer have been completed; (4) No company shall make an offer or invitation under this section unless a special resolution has been passed by the company; (5) The offer must be accompanied by a private placement offer letter; (6) Monies received must be kept in a separate bank account. Violation of these conditions makes the offer void and the company and its promoters are liable to a penalty.
Q003: Under the SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) Regulations 2012, an Alternative Investment Fund (AIF) means any fund established or incorporated in India in the form of a trust or a company or a limited liability partnership or a body corporate which is a privately pooled investment vehicle that collects funds from sophisticated investors, whether Indian or foreign, for investing in accordance with a defined investment policy for the benefit of its investors. AIF does NOT include funds covered under the SEBI (Mutual Funds) Regulations 1996, SEBI (Collective Investment Schemes) Regulations 1999, or any other regulations of SEBI. AIFs are classified into three categories: Category I (venture capital, SME funds, social venture, infrastructure), Category II (private equity, debt funds, fund of funds), and Category III (employs diverse trading strategies, may use leverage).
Q004: Under Section 380 of the Companies Act 2013, every foreign company shall, within thirty days of establishing a place of business in India, deliver to the Registrar for registration: (1) A certified copy of the charter, statutes, or memorandum and articles of the company or other instrument constituting or defining the constitution of the company; (2) The full address of the registered or principal office of the company; (3) A list of the directors and secretary of the company with specified particulars; (4) The name and address or addresses of one or more persons resident in India authorised to accept service of process and notices; (5) The full address of the principal place of business in India; (6) Particulars of opening and closing of a place of business in India. Every foreign company must also file annual accounts in the prescribed form.
Q005: Under Section 92 of the Companies Act 2013, every company shall prepare an annual return in the prescribed form containing particulars as they stood on the close of the financial year. The annual return must be filed with the Registrar within sixty days from the date on which the annual general meeting is held or where no AGM is held, within sixty days from the date on which the AGM should have been held. The annual return must contain particulars including: registered address, principal business activities, share capital and debentures, indebtedness, details of members and debenture-holders, details of directors, key managerial personnel, meetings, remuneration of directors and KMP, penalties or punishments, and related party transactions. A company's annual return must be signed by a director and the company secretary, or where there is no company secretary, by a company secretary in practice.
Q006: Under Section 42 of the Companies Act 2013, private placement is subject to the following conditions and restrictions: (1) Offer limited to 200 persons per financial year — no offer/invitation to more than two hundred persons in aggregate; (2) No public advertisement — the offer cannot be made through any media, marketing, or distribution channels; (3) Prior recording of names — offer may be made only to persons whose names are recorded by the company; (4) Preceding allotment must be complete — no fresh offer until previous allotment is done; (5) Special resolution required — must be passed by shareholders before making any private placement; (6) Private placement offer letter (Form PAS-4) must be sent; (7) Application money must be kept in a separate bank account and cannot be used unless allotment is complete and return of allotment is filed with the Registrar; (8) Shares must be allotted within sixty days of receipt of application money; (9) Return of allotment must be filed with the Registrar within fifteen days of allotment.
Q007: Under Section 166 of the Companies Act 2013, a director must: act in accordance with articles; act in good faith for the benefit of members, employees, community and environment; exercise due care, skill and diligence; exercise independent judgment; not create situations of conflict of interest; not seek undue gain. Under Section 167, office of a director becomes vacant if they incur disqualifications under Section 164, fail to attend three consecutive board meetings without leave of absence, or act in contravention of Section 184 (disclosure of interest). Consequences of failure include: (1) Penalty of not less than one lakh rupees and not more than five lakh rupees under Section 166(7) for breach of duties; (2) Vacation of office under Section 167; (3) Liability for breach of fiduciary duty including D&O insurance obligations under the company's Code of Business Ethics; (4) Civil and criminal liability for transactions entered into in conflict of interest without proper disclosure.
Q008: Under the SEBI (Alternative Investment Funds) Regulations 2012, to register as an AIF: (1) The AIF must be established in India as a trust, company, LLP, or body corporate; (2) It must be a privately pooled investment vehicle; (3) Application must be made to SEBI in Form A with prescribed fee; (4) The applicant must have minimum corpus of twenty crore rupees (ten crore for angel funds); (5) Minimum investment amount from each investor is one crore rupees (twenty-five lakh for employees/directors); (6) The applicant must not be a company incorporated outside India; (7) The manager or sponsor must have adequate experience and key investment team must have adequate experience in fund management or portfolio management; (8) The applicant must be a fit and proper person. For a partnership firm specifically, the AIF must be established under the Indian Partnership Act 1932 and each partner must independently satisfy applicable eligibility conditions.
Q009: Under Section 206 of the Companies Act 2013, the Registrar or an officer authorised by the Central Government may call for information or explanation from a company or its officers. Under Section 207, the Central Government may direct inspection of books and papers of a company by an inspector appointed for the purpose. The powers include: (1) Requiring a company to produce its books of account and other records for inspection; (2) Examining directors, officers, and employees under oath; (3) Seizing books of account, financial statements, and other documents; (4) Inspecting books of account at the company's registered office. Inspections may be ordered suo motu by the Central Government or on receipt of application from not less than one hundred members or members holding not less than one-tenth of the total voting power. The inspector has powers of a civil court for the purpose of inspection.
Q010: Under Section 152 of the Companies Act 2013, every director must be appointed by the company in a general meeting, unless the articles provide otherwise. Directors are appointed for a term not exceeding five years at a time and are eligible for reappointment. Under Section 164, a person is disqualified from being appointed as a director if: (1) He is of unsound mind; (2) He is an undischarged insolvent; (3) He has applied to be adjudicated as an insolvent; (4) He has been convicted of any offence involving moral turpitude or otherwise and sentenced to imprisonment for not less than six months and five years have not elapsed; (5) He has not paid any call in respect of shares held by him; (6) He has been convicted of an offence dealing with related party transactions under Section 188; (7) He has not complied with Section 152(3) regarding DIN. Under Section 151, a listed company may have a director elected by the small shareholders (holding shares of nominal value of not more than twenty thousand rupees).
Q011: A DPIIT-recognised startup that issues securities must comply with both sets of regulations: Under DPIIT Guidelines, the entity must be incorporated as a private limited company, registered partnership firm, or LLP; must have been incorporated for no more than ten years; must have annual turnover not exceeding one hundred crore rupees; and must be working towards innovation, development or improvement of products/services/processes. Under SEBI regulations applicable to startups issuing securities: (1) If issuing to alternative investment funds, the startup must comply with SEBI (AIF) Regulations 2012 requirements for the specific category of AIF; (2) If issuing equity shares or convertible securities to more than 200 investors, it constitutes a public offer requiring compliance with SEBI (ICDR) Regulations 2018; (3) For private placements, the startup must comply with Section 42 of the Companies Act 2013. DPIIT recognition does not exempt the entity from SEBI filing requirements for public securities issuances.
Q012: When a company makes a private placement to an Alternative Investment Fund, the following rules apply: Under the Companies Act 2013 Section 42, the company must: record the AIF as one of the select persons before the offer; ensure total offerees (including the AIF) do not exceed 200 in the financial year; pass a special resolution; send a private placement offer letter in Form PAS-4; keep application money in a separate bank account; allot within 60 days and file return of allotment within 15 days. Under SEBI AIF Regulations 2012, the AIF receiving the private placement: must be a registered AIF with SEBI; must invest in accordance with its investment policy and stated category; for Category II AIF investing in unlisted securities, must ensure minimum investment per investor of one crore rupees; the AIF manager must conduct due diligence on the investee company. The private placement to an AIF is exempt from the definition of a public offer provided the conditions under Section 42 are strictly followed.
Q013: Under the Companies Act 2013, directors' corporate governance obligations include: Section 166 — duty to act in good faith, exercise independent judgment, avoid conflicts; Section 184 — mandatory disclosure of interest in contracts/arrangements; Section 173 — participation in board meetings; Section 149 — requirements for independent directors on listed companies; Section 177 — audit committee requirements. Under SEBI Listing Obligations and Disclosure Requirements Regulations (for listed companies), directors are bound by additional obligations including: composition requirements for board committees; mandatory disclosure of related party transactions; code of conduct and ethics requirements; restrictions on insider trading under SEBI (Prohibition of Insider Trading) Regulations; obligations to make timely disclosures of price-sensitive information. The Companies Act and SEBI frameworks are complementary — Companies Act sets the baseline, while SEBI regulations impose stricter standards on listed company directors.
Q014: Under DPIIT Guidelines for startup recognition, entities formed through merger, demerger, acquisition, or amalgamation may be affected as follows: (1) Merger/Demerger/Acquisition/Amalgamation — Resultant entity or entities formed due to such restructuring may apply for fresh DPIIT recognition if they meet all eligibility conditions independently (incorporated within 10 years, turnover under Rs 100 crore, working towards innovation); (2) The original DPIIT recognition does not automatically transfer to the resultant entity; (3) Compromise/Arrangement — Entities formed due to compromise/arrangement as provided under the Companies Act may similarly apply for recognition afresh. Under the Companies Act 2013, restructuring transactions must comply with: Section 230-232 for mergers/amalgamations (requiring NCLT approval); Section 234 for cross-border mergers; Sections 391-394 for schemes of arrangement. The NCLT proceedings and compliance with the Companies Act are prerequisites before a restructured entity can apply to DPIIT for recognition.
Q015: To register as a Category II Alternative Investment Fund under SEBI AIF Regulations 2012: (1) Application: File application with SEBI in Form A with application fee; (2) Entity structure: Must be established as trust, company, LLP, or body corporate in India; (3) Corpus requirement: Minimum corpus of twenty crore rupees; (4) Investor minimum: Minimum investment of one crore rupees per investor (twenty-five lakh for directors/employees of the AIF/manager); (5) Maximum investors: Not more than one thousand investors; (6) Category II AIFs include private equity funds, debt funds, and fund of funds that do not fall under Category I or III; (7) Category II AIFs may invest in unlisted securities, listed securities with lock-in, real estate, and structured credit; (8) Category II AIFs cannot use leverage except for day-to-day operational purposes; (9) Disclosure obligations: Must submit placement memorandum to SEBI before accepting funds; (10) Reporting: Quarterly reports to SEBI and annual reports to investors.
Q016: Under DPIIT Guidelines for startup recognition, the conditions under which a recognised startup may retain its recognition upon a name change are: (1) The entity must apply to DPIIT to update its recognition certificate with the new name; (2) The underlying legal entity (company/LLP/partnership) must be the same — only the name changes, not the legal form or incorporation details; (3) The entity must continue to meet all eligibility criteria for startup recognition: incorporated for no more than ten years from date of original incorporation, turnover not exceeding one hundred crore rupees in any financial year, and working towards innovation/development/improvement; (4) The name change must be registered with the Registrar of Companies (for companies) or the relevant authority (for LLPs/partnerships) under the applicable law; (5) Any tax benefits, exemptions, or certificates obtained under the original name must be updated with the relevant authorities. A mere name change does not constitute a new incorporation and the original date of incorporation is retained for computing the ten-year eligibility period.
Q017: Under SEBI AIF Regulations 2012, a partnership firm seeking registration as an AIF must satisfy: (1) The AIF must be set up under the Indian Partnership Act 1932; (2) Each partner must independently satisfy the eligibility conditions for being an investor in the AIF; (3) The partnership firm must have a defined investment policy stated in its placement memorandum; (4) Minimum corpus of twenty crore rupees must be raised; (5) Each partner's investment must be at least one crore rupees (except for partners who are directors or employees of the AIF/manager, for whom the minimum is twenty-five lakh rupees); (6) The manager or sponsor of the AIF (who may be a partner or a separately appointed entity) must have adequate experience in fund management; (7) The partnership must not be established for a period exceeding close-ended fund tenure; (8) The partnership firm must comply with all applicable laws including the Indian Partnership Act requirements for registration, maintenance of accounts, and audit.
Q018: Under Section 184 of the Companies Act 2013, a director must disclose their interest in the following circumstances: (1) At the first board meeting after appointment, the director must disclose all companies, bodies corporate, firms, or other associations in which they are directly or indirectly interested; (2) At the first board meeting of every financial year thereafter, a fresh disclosure must be made; (3) When a contract or arrangement is proposed, in which the director is directly or indirectly interested, they must disclose the nature of their concern or interest at the board meeting where the contract/arrangement is considered. Consequences of non-disclosure: (1) Any contract or arrangement entered into without proper disclosure shall be voidable at the option of the company; (2) The director shall be liable to a penalty of one lakh rupees; (3) The director must not participate or vote in the board meeting where the related contract/arrangement is being discussed. Under Section 188, related party transactions require additional approval by special resolution (for listed companies) or ordinary resolution where the interested director is not entitled to vote.
Q019: Under DPIIT Guidelines for startup recognition, the following conditions apply when a DPIIT-recognised startup incorporates an additional entity: (1) Incorporating additional entities with similar address: DPIIT may scrutinize such entities to determine if they constitute a new genuine startup or are merely shell entities created to misuse tax benefits; (2) Common directorship/partnership: Recognition of an entity having common directors or designated partners with an existing recognised startup is subject to DPIIT review — the new entity must independently demonstrate innovation, development, or improvement of a product/service/process distinct from the existing entity; (3) The new entity must independently apply for DPIIT recognition and meet all eligibility criteria in its own right; (4) The new entity must not be formed merely by splitting or reconstructing an existing business; (5) DPIIT reserves the right to revoke recognition if it determines that recognition was obtained by misrepresentation or the entity no longer meets eligibility criteria. Both entities must maintain separate accounts and demonstrate separate and genuine business activities.
Q020: Under Section 151 of the Companies Act 2013, a listed company may have one director elected by the small shareholders. Small shareholders are defined as shareholders holding shares of nominal value of not more than twenty thousand rupees. Conditions and eligibility: (1) The listed company may, if prescribed, have one director to be elected by such small shareholders in such manner and with such terms and conditions as may be prescribed; (2) A person shall be eligible for appointment as a small shareholder director only if: they are a small shareholder themselves; a notice has been given of the intention to propose such a person as director, signed by at least one thousand small shareholders or one-tenth of the total number of small shareholders, whichever is lower; (3) The director elected by small shareholders is not liable to retire by rotation; (4) The tenure of such director shall not exceed a period of three consecutive years; (5) Such director shall not be eligible for re-appointment after the expiry of the tenure; (6) The director shall be an independent director and must satisfy the conditions for independence under Section 149(6).
